text: During the phase of isolation of the segmental arterial branch for the middle lobe, we are attempting to separate the artery from the adventitia.
Unfortunately, the tip of the robotic hook caused damage to the vascular surface, leading to bleeding.
The blood loss was modest and in this case we proceeded to introduce a gauze with which we exerted a compression of about 15 minutes on the injured artery.
After the compression the bleeding appeared to be resolved.
We therefore completed the isolation of the arterial branch very gently and dissected the vessel by means of a vascular stapler.
The second case on the other hand describes a bleeding occurred during a right upper lobectomy.
The mediastinal arterial trunk was damaged during the incision of the mediastinal pleura for pulmonary artery isolation.
Bleeding was controlled using the robotic carrière used as a vascular clamp.

By keeping the damaged vascular surface clamped, we were able to place Hemlock upstream and downstream of the lesion by checking and resolving the bleeding without the need to convert the surgery into thoracotomy.
For greater security, we then section the artery with robotic scissors.
In the third case, we describe another complication that occurred during the vascular section by the means of the steady plug.
After isolating the arterial branch for the middle lobe, we used a robotic stapler for the section.
Unfortunately, not all of the vessel lumen was included in the stapler and therefore the residual portion started to bleed.
We then succeeded in subsequently isolating the stump by positioning hammerlock and thus controlling the bleeding without converting the case into a thoracotomy.
The patient was a woman with a very high BMI.
She had a fairly high diaphragm already evident in the CT stand shown prior.
The first step of standard was the preparation of the superior pulmonary vein.
This is the most recent recorded video.
Now in the dissection phase, we always use the curved bipolar dissector and no longer use the cautery hook.
After the isolation of the vein for the medium, we found difficulty in inserting the stapler due to the high position of the diaphragm.
The first surgeon used the bipolar curve dissector to lower the diaphragm, but this caused a hole in the diaphragm itself, consistently with the liver.
To have good control of the hemostasis, it was necessary to convert the operation into thoracotomy, open the diaphragm and practice hemostasis on the liver.
In the fifth case, we're concerned with a right upper lobectomy in a patient suffering from pulmonary adenocarcinoma.
The incident occurred during the isolation of the mediastinal arterial trunk while it was proceeding to separate from the superior pulmonary vein.
During the separation of artery from the adventitia, too much energy was applied on the cardiac and this resulted in a tear on the lumen of the vessel.
The strategy as always is the same to try and keep calm and clear the field by inhaling all the blood.
The second step is to try and clamp the vessel with the caddy air upstream and downstream.

The presence of the utility incision that we always practice in robotic lobectomies is especially advantageous in these cases and in fact has allowed us to open two clamps to secure the patient and practice urgent thoracotomy.
